source: Model answers for 6 official WSET L3 wines

primary_descriptors.specificity_rule: Use specific fruit names (e.g., 'red cherry') not generic terms ('fruity')
primary_descriptors.quantity_rule: 3-5 primary descriptors is optimal; listing too many reduces impact; too few loses marks

secondary_descriptors.attribution_optional: Can state 'oak influence gives notes of vanilla, cedar' (as in Bordeaux model answer) or just list without attribution
secondary_descriptors.rule: For complex wines, at least one secondary descriptor is required for full marks

tertiary_descriptors.rule: For premium/aged wines, minimum 1 tertiary descriptor required. Missing tertiary is the #1 mark loss for distinction candidates

wrong_category: using 'honey' as primary when it is tertiary
using 'vanilla' as primary when it is secondary (oak)

category_assignment_rules.primary: From the grape itself or alcoholic fermentation: fresh fruit, flowers, herbs, spices from grape
category_assignment_rules.secondary: From winemaking choices post-AF: oak ageing (vanilla/cedar/smoke), MLF (butter/cream), lees contact (biscuit/bread/brioche)
category_assignment_rules.tertiary: From bottle or oxidative ageing: honey, petrol, leather, tobacco, forest floor, dried fruit

common_confusion_pairs.note: Can be secondary (oak/lees) or tertiary (bottle); context determines
Tertiary (bottle ageing) for whites; NOT primary
Primary if fresh dried fruit (e.g., raisins from grape); tertiary if aged fruit character
